summary: The main claim is: I am imagining a system that gets as its goal a target state of the world, that should be reached.
The main claim is: Last year, we taught an AGISF course to Master's students at a French university (ENS Ulm).
The course consisted of 10 sessions, each lasting 2 hours, and drew inspiration from the official AGISF curriculum.
The main claim is: Participants were asked how much they agreed with 50 statements about what AGI labs should do.
The main claim is: Summary by OpenAI: We use GPT-4 to automatically write explanations for the behavior of neurons in large language models and to score those explanations.
We release a dataset of these (imperfect) explanations and scores for every neuron in GPT-2.
The main claim is: A lot of the criticism of LLMs has centered on how it’s not able to pursue its own goals, and I want to argue that that won’t be a limitation for very long.
Instead, I think it’ll lead to LLMs being subtly more engaging, because they’ll be trained to keep humans talking.
The main claim is: This means it is very likely that we will have to live with AGI/ASI once we build it for real.
By default, this means that people should mostly ignore the worst or best outcomes if they want to predict how a crisis or x-risk will go.
The main claim is: We need more uncorrelated ("diverse") approaches to alignment.
"I'm a smart person, therefore my solution should be sufficiently-complicated." I think similar reasons of vanity (and related social-status) reasons are holding back some AI alignment progress.
The main claim is: A little more on the details of the Ideal Bureaucracy Structure below.
The whole "bureaucracy as a robust agent-agnostic process" thing?
The main claim is: Alphabet, Microsoft and Facebook all could go on just fine if deep learning turns out to be a mirage.
